behaviors.description: Conversation history passed to the LLM is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when the full history exceeds the context window.
behaviors.justification: This is a pure data transformation function with no I/O or external dependencies. The failure (history not trimmed) is caught by verifying the function's output directly. Mocking the LLM is unnecessary; the trimming algorithm itself is testable in isolation by passing a history list and checking that exactly the last 10 items are returned.
behaviors.test_description: Pass a conversation history with 15+ messages to the trim_conversation_history() function and verify that exactly 10 messages are returned and they are the most recent ones. Assert len(trimmed) == 10 and trimmed == history[-10:].

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that the LLM generates off-topic or irrelevant content. This only manifests with a real LLM; a mocked LLM would always return valid, on-topic responses regardless of the prompt quality or instruction adherence. The LLM's ability to stay topically relevant is non-deterministic and depends on the actual model's behavior, temperature settings, and prompting quality. A pure mock cannot reveal topical failures.
behaviors.test_description: Call the response generation function with a real LLM and a specific, detailed customer question (e.g., 'What is your return policy for electronics?'). Verify the response contains on-topic keywords or concepts directly related to the question (e.g., 'return', 'policy', 'refund' for the example) and does not consist of generic placeholders or unrelated content.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that the tone classifier (an LLM-based component) incorrectly judges formality — either it flags a casual response as formal (false positive) or misses a clearly formal response (false negative). These failures only occur with a real classifier; a mocked tone checker always returns the 'correct' verdict. The classifier's accuracy is model-dependent and non-deterministic. A pure mock hides the real failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Call the response generation and tone-checking pipeline with a real LLM tone classifier. Provide a customer-tier setting (casual) and a response that should trigger the tone check (or mock the initial LLM to return a distinctly formal response). Verify that the tone classifier flags it and regeneration is triggered, and the regenerated response has a lower formality score than the initial response.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: The failure is at the database level: data not being saved, timestamps being incorrect, or ordering being corrupted. These failures are invisible to a mocked database. A real database (or realistic in-memory database like SQLite) is required to catch persistence failures, index issues, or timestamp handling bugs. There is no LLM dependency here; this is purely about data persistence.
behaviors.test_description: Call the persist_exchange() function with a complete exchange (user message, initial response, regeneration list). Read the record back from a real or in-memory database and verify: (1) all parts (user message, responses, regenerations) are present and in order, (2) timestamps are set and correct (within 1 second of the current time), (3) the regeneration timestamps are after the initial response timestamp.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: The failure is at the API/messaging level: the customer receives the wrong response (the rejected draft instead of the regenerated one, or both are sent). This is only testable by hitting the actual API endpoint or checking what was persisted for the customer in the database. You can mock the LLM components (initial generation and tone check) to control the scenario and force regeneration, but you must verify the user-facing output (API response or database record) is correct. Mocking at the logic level would hide the failure of sending the wrong response to the customer.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the initial LLM to return a recognizable formal response (e.g., 'I hereby acknowledge...') and mock the tone checker to flag it as too formal. Call the chat API endpoint and verify the response contains the regenerated message (less formal) and not the mocked formal response. Additionally, query the database for the exchange record and verify the 'sent_to_customer' field contains the regenerated response, not the initial formal one.
